Title: Auxin Biosynthesis: A Simple Two-Step Pathway Converts Tryptophan to Indole-3-Acetic Acid in Plants
Journal: Cold Spring Harbor Perspectives in Biology

Common_Genes.Applied_Promoters: YUC promoter (used to drive bacterial iaaM for rescue); overexpression promoters not specified in excerpt
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Manipulating YUC family expression enables temporal/spatial tuning of endogenous IAA for plant architecture and development. Best deployed with tissue-specific promoters because YUC genes are rate-limiting and broad overexpression causes auxin overproduction phenotypes and pleiotropic developmental defects. Functional redundancy means multiplex editing/stacking of family members may be required.
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: YUC family is essential for auxin biosynthesis; increased YUC activity elevates IAA and causes auxin overproduction, whereas combined loss of multiple YUCs reduces auxin and causes severe developmental defects.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Overexpression; higher-order loss-of-function mutants; rescue of yuc mutants with iaaM driven by a YUC promoter; IPA/IAA metabolite measurements; in vitro biochemical assays
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Shade avoidance assays; ethylene response; NPA treatment in roots
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Family members are temporally and spatially regulated; YUC1/YUC2/YUC4/YUC6 mainly function in shoots, while YUC3/YUC5/YUC7/YUC8/YUC9 mainly function in roots
Common_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: TAA family (same pathway; genetic and biochemical dependency)
Common_Genes.Omics_Data: Metabolite profiling of IPA/IAA mentioned; no accession reported
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: YUCs were previously proposed to act in a tryptamine branch, but this review concludes they act downstream of TAA in the IPA branch. Family redundancy explains why single mutants are often weak.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Constitutive overexpression causes classic auxin overproduction traits such as long hypocotyls and epinastic cotyledons; severe higher-order mutants show defects in embryogenesis, seedling growth, flowers, and vascular patterning.
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. YUC genes encode essential auxin-biosynthetic enzymes in the main Trp-dependent pathway. 2. YUC overexpression increases auxin, while higher-order yuc mutants are auxin-deficient with severe developmental defects. 3. YUCs act downstream of TAAs to convert IPA toward IAA and represent the rate-limiting step in the plant pathway.
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Local sites of auxin biosynthesis in shoots and roots
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Essential regulator of plant growth and development, including embryogenesis, seedling growth, flower development, vascular patterning, shade avoidance, and root responses

Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: TAA family can be targeted to modulate precursor supply for endogenous IAA production. Tissue- and stage-specific engineering is preferable because reduced TAA activity causes auxin deficiency and developmental defects, and full pathway output also depends on downstream YUC activity.
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: TAA family is essential for auxin biosynthesis; loss of TAA1/TAR genes lowers IPA and auxin, producing partial auxin deficiency and multiple developmental defects.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant analysis of taa1 tar1 tar2; phenotypic rescue by exogenous auxin; genetic interaction with yuc mutants; IPA measurements; in vitro enzyme assays
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Temporally and spatially regulated; Arabidopsis seedlings appear to use the same set of TAA genes for both root and shoot development
Common_Genes.Omics_Data: Metabolite profiling of IPA/IAA mentioned; no accession reported
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: TAA genes were identified from screens for altered responses to shade, ethylene, and NPA. The TAA-catalyzed reaction is coupled to amino acid metabolism via transfer of the Trp amino group to an alpha-keto acid.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Reduced activity causes auxin-deficiency developmental defects; pathway manipulation may affect other amino acid/alpha-keto acid pathways because the reaction is a transamination.
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. TAA genes catalyze the first step of the main auxin pathway from Trp to IPA. 2. taa mutant combinations are auxin-deficient and phenocopy major yuc developmental defects. 3. TAA activity is required for YUC-mediated auxin overproduction, placing TAAs upstream of YUCs.
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Local sites of auxin biosynthesis in shoots and roots
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Essential regulator of plant growth and development, including embryogenesis, shade avoidance, and root growth responses

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: TAA1 is useful for tuning auxin precursor supply, but engineering should consider redundancy with TAR genes and dependence on downstream YUC activity. Tissue-specific modification may alter organ development with fewer whole-plant defects than constitutive perturbation.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Transfers the amino group from tryptophan to an alpha-keto acid such as pyruvate, producing indole-3-pyruvate (IPA) and a corresponding amino acid
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes IAA biosynthesis by generating IPA precursor; reduced TAA1 activity contributes to auxin deficiency and developmental defects
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Genetic mutant analysis; partial rescue by exogenous auxin; IPA metabolite measurements; in vitro biochemical assay showing Trp to IPA conversion
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Forms immediate precursor of target product
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Shade; ethylene; NPA responses in roots
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Expressed in seedlings and contributes to both root and shoot development; temporally and spatially regulated
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Metabolite profiling of IPA/IAA; no accession reported
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: TAA1 was identified in a genetic screen related to shade/ethylene/NPA responses. The reaction may influence additional pathways through amino acceptor metabolism.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Loss causes developmental defects; perturbation may influence other alpha-keto acid/amino acid pools
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Substrate: Tryptophan (Trp) plus alpha-keto acid acceptor such as pyruvate
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Essential upstream step; not identified here as the main rate-limiting step
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. TAA1 converts Trp to IPA in the first committed reaction of the main IAA pathway. 2. Mutations lower IPA and auxin, causing developmental defects. 3. TAA1 acts upstream of YUC-dependent conversion of IPA to IAA.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Local sites of auxin biosynthesis in shoots and roots

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: TAR1 may need to be stacked with TAA1/TAR2 edits because of redundancy. Useful for modulating local auxin production, but constitutive loss can impair development.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Aminotransferase step converting tryptophan to indole-3-pyruvate (IPA) using an alpha-keto acid acceptor
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Contributes redundantly to IPA and IAA biosynthesis; loss together with other TAA family genes causes auxin-deficiency phenotypes
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Higher-order mutant analysis; IPA metabolite measurements; pathway genetics
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Forms immediate precursor of target product
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Shade; ethylene; NPA-related assays through family mutants
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Temporally and spatially regulated in seedlings; contributes with other TAAs in root and shoot development
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Metabolite profiling mentioned; no accession reported
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Acts redundantly with TAA1 and TAR2; family-level rather than single-gene effects dominate developmental phenotypes.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Multiplex disruption can severely impair development
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Essential upstream step; not established as rate-limiting
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. TAR1 is one of the TAA-family enzymes supplying IPA for IAA biosynthesis. 2. It functions redundantly with TAA1 and TAR2. 3. Family inactivation causes auxin-deficiency developmental defects.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Local sites of auxin biosynthesis in shoots and roots

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: TAR2 is a useful lever for local auxin engineering, especially in combination with TAA1 or YUC manipulation. Because of redundancy and strong developmental effects in combinations, deployment should favor spatially restricted modulation.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Aminotransferase step converting tryptophan to indole-3-pyruvate (IPA) using an alpha-keto acid acceptor
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Supports IAA formation through IPA production; mutation contributes to auxin-deficient developmental phenotypes, especially in combination with other TAA genes
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Higher-order mutant analysis; phenocopy with yuc combinations; IPA metabolite measurements; pathway genetics
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Forms immediate precursor of target product
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Shade; ethylene; NPA-related assays through family mutants
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Temporally and spatially regulated in seedlings; used in both root and shoot development
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Metabolite profiling mentioned; no accession reported
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: taa1 tar2 combinations mimic characteristic yuc interaction phenotypes in pid and npy1 backgrounds, supporting shared pathway membership.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Combined disruption with other TAA genes causes severe developmental defects
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Essential upstream step; not established as rate-limiting
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. TAR2 participates in the first enzymatic step from Trp to IPA. 2. It acts redundantly with other TAA-family genes in auxin biosynthesis. 3. Genetic interactions mirror those of yuc mutants, placing TAR2 in the same pathway upstream of YUCs.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Local sites of auxin biosynthesis in shoots and roots

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: YUC promoter used in rescue experiments; overexpression promoters not specified
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: YUC1 is a strong engineering target for increasing local auxin biosynthesis, especially in shoots. Because YUC catalysis is rate-limiting, moderate or tissue-specific expression is recommended to avoid severe auxin overproduction. Stacking with TAA can further enhance IAA production.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Oxygen- and NADPH-dependent conversion of indole-3-pyruvate (IPA) to indole-3-acetic acid (IAA)
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes IAA production; overexpression causes auxin overproduction, while loss in combination with other YUCs causes severe auxin-deficient defects
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Overexpression phenotypes; higher-order mutant analysis; rescue by YUC-promoter::iaaM; IPA accumulation in yuc mutants; in vitro biochemical assays using IPA as substrate
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Mainly functions in shoots; YUC1 is among the main shoot YUC genes
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Metabolite profiling of IPA/IAA; no accession reported
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: YUC1 had been linked earlier to a tryptamine route, but this review places it in the IPA branch. yuc1 yuc4 and higher-order combinations reveal strong redundancy.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Constitutive overexpression causes long hypocotyls and epinastic cotyledons; loss with other YUCs disrupts embryogenesis and organ development
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Rate-limiting step in plant auxin biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. YUC1 catalyzes conversion of IPA to IAA in the main auxin pathway. 2. It acts downstream of TAA-family enzymes and is part of the rate-limiting step. 3. Altered YUC1 dosage strongly changes auxin levels and development.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Sites of auxin biosynthesis, especially shoots

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: YUC2 is a candidate for shoot-targeted modulation of auxin biosynthesis, but redundancy suggests stacking with other shoot YUCs may be needed. Avoid constitutive high expression because YUC enzymes are rate-limiting and dosage sensitive.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Oxygen- and NADPH-dependent conversion of indole-3-pyruvate (IPA) to IAA
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Contributes to auxin production in shoots as part of the YUC family
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Family overexpression and higher-order mutant analysis; biochemical family assignment
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Metabolite profiling at family level; no accession reported
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Named as one of the major shoot YUCs in Arabidopsis seedlings.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Likely pleiotropic developmental effects if strongly misexpressed, as seen for YUC overexpression generally
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Part of the rate-limiting YUC step
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. YUC2 is one of the major shoot YUC enzymes for IPA-to-IAA conversion. 2. It functions redundantly with other YUC family members. 3. The YUC-catalyzed step is rate-limiting in plant auxin biosynthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Sites of auxin biosynthesis in shoots

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: YUC3 is suitable for root-focused auxin engineering. Multiplex strategies with other root YUCs may be required because family redundancy is substantial.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Oxygen- and NADPH-dependent conversion of indole-3-pyruvate (IPA) to IAA
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Contributes to root auxin biosynthesis; loss in quintuple root YUC combinations causes auxin-related root phenotypes including ethylene and NPA resistance
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Higher-order mutant analysis (yucQ); metabolite profiling; family biochemical assignment
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Metabolite profiling at family level; no accession reported
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Part of root-specific YUC set (YUC3/5/7/8/9) in seedlings.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Combined perturbation with other root YUCs alters root developmental and response phenotypes
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Part of the rate-limiting YUC step
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. YUC3 contributes to IPA-to-IAA conversion in roots. 2. It acts redundantly with YUC5/7/8/9. 3. Root YUC combinations phenocopy taa root phenotypes.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Controls root growth and developmental responses

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: YUC promoter used in rescue context; overexpression promoters not specified
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: YUC4 is an effective target for shoot developmental engineering and can be paired with YUC1. Tissue-specific deployment is critical because YUC4 perturbation affects cotyledon initiation and shade responses in sensitized backgrounds.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Oxygen- and NADPH-dependent conversion of indole-3-pyruvate (IPA) to IAA
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes shoot auxin biosynthesis; combined loss with YUC1 and others causes embryo and shoot defects, while overexpression causes auxin overproduction
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Overexpression; double and higher-order mutant analysis; iaaM rescue using YUC promoter; phenocopy in developmental assays
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Metabolite profiling at family level; no accession reported
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: yuc1 yuc4 combinations are central genetic tools demonstrating pathway redundancy and shoot functions.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Misexpression likely causes pleiotropic auxin overproduction; combined loss disrupts cotyledon initiation and shade avoidance
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Part of the rate-limiting YUC step
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. YUC4 is a major shoot YUC enzyme for IAA biosynthesis. 2. Genetic interactions with YUC1 reveal strong redundancy in development. 3. It participates in the rate-limiting IPA-to-IAA step.

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: YUC5 can be used for root-local auxin engineering, likely in combination with YUC3/7/8/9 because of redundancy.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Oxygen- and NADPH-dependent conversion of indole-3-pyruvate (IPA) to IAA
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Contributes redundantly to root auxin biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Metabolite profiling at family level; no accession reported
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Part of root YUC quintuple mutant set that phenocopies taa root responses.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Combined disruption alters root developmental responses
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Part of the rate-limiting YUC step
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. YUC5 contributes to root IPA-to-IAA conversion. 2. It functions redundantly with other root YUC genes. 3. Root YUC combinations explain taa-like phenotypes.

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: YUC6 is a shoot YUC candidate for engineering local IAA production; dosage control is important due to rate-limiting activity.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Oxygen- and NADPH-dependent conversion of indole-3-pyruvate (IPA) to IAA
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Contributes to shoot auxin biosynthesis as part of the YUC family
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Metabolite profiling at family level; no accession reported
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Listed as one of the major shoot YUC genes in seedlings.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Likely pleiotropic if overexpressed strongly, as for YUCs generally
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Part of the rate-limiting YUC step
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. YUC6 is one of the major shoot YUC enzymes. 2. It contributes to the rate-limiting IPA-to-IAA step. 3. It acts redundantly with other YUC family members.

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: YUC7 is a root YUC candidate for local auxin adjustment; effective manipulation likely requires multiplex control with other root YUCs.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Oxygen- and NADPH-dependent conversion of indole-3-pyruvate (IPA) to IAA
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Contributes to root auxin biosynthesis as part of the redundant YUC root module
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Metabolite profiling at family level; no accession reported
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Part of root YUC module in seedlings.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Combined perturbation with other root YUCs affects root responses
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Part of the rate-limiting YUC step
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. YUC7 contributes to root IAA biosynthesis. 2. It functions redundantly within the root YUC module. 3. Its pathway position is the rate-limiting IPA-to-IAA step.

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: YUC8 is useful for root-local engineering of auxin biosynthesis, but redundancy necessitates combined targeting and careful developmental monitoring.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Oxygen- and NADPH-dependent conversion of indole-3-pyruvate (IPA) to IAA
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Contributes to root auxin biosynthesis; included in root YUC mutant combinations that phenocopy taa root phenotypes
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Metabolite profiling at family level; no accession reported
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Part of yucQ root mutant set.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Combined perturbation affects root growth and hormone/drug responses
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Part of the rate-limiting YUC step
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. YUC8 is a root-expressed member of the YUC auxin biosynthesis module. 2. It contributes redundantly to IPA-to-IAA conversion. 3. Root YUC mutant combinations phenocopy taa mutants.

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: YUC9 can be used in root-specific auxin engineering but likely requires stacking with YUC3/5/7/8 because of redundancy.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Oxygen- and NADPH-dependent conversion of indole-3-pyruvate (IPA) to IAA
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Contributes redundantly to root auxin biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Metabolite profiling at family level; no accession reported
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Part of root YUC module in seedlings.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Combined perturbation affects root developmental responses
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Part of the rate-limiting YUC step
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. YUC9 contributes to root IAA biosynthesis. 2. It functions redundantly with other root YUC genes. 3. Its enzymatic position is the YUC rate-limiting step.

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: YUC10 can contribute to embryo/development-related auxin engineering, but redundancy with YUC1/4/11 means combinatorial designs are needed and pleiotropy risk is high.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Oxygen- and NADPH-dependent conversion of indole-3-pyruvate (IPA) to IAA
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Contributes to auxin biosynthesis during embryo development; mutation in combination with YUC1/YUC4/YUC11 causes basal embryo defects
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Implied developmental/embryo contribution; precise pattern not detailed in excerpt
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Not reported beyond family-level metabolite profiling
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Included in yuc1 yuc4 yuc10 yuc11 quadruple mutant defective in basal embryo formation.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Combinatorial loss causes severe embryogenesis defects
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Part of the rate-limiting YUC step
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. YUC10 is part of a YUC combination essential for embryonic auxin biosynthesis. 2. It acts redundantly with other YUCs. 3. The YUC reaction is the rate-limiting step from IPA to IAA.

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: YUC11 may be used in developmental auxin engineering in combination with other embryo/shoot YUC genes, but high pleiotropy risk demands precise expression control.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Oxygen- and NADPH-dependent conversion of indole-3-pyruvate (IPA) to IAA
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Contributes to auxin biosynthesis during embryo development in combination with YUC1/YUC4/YUC10
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Implied developmental/embryo contribution; precise pattern not detailed in excerpt
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Not reported beyond family-level metabolite profiling
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Included in yuc1 yuc4 yuc10 yuc11 quadruple mutant with embryo defects.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Combinatorial loss causes severe embryogenesis defects
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Part of the rate-limiting YUC step
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. YUC11 functions redundantly in embryonic auxin biosynthesis. 2. It belongs to the YUC rate-limiting step converting IPA to IAA. 3. Combined loss with other YUCs causes strong developmental defects.

Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: Indole-3-acetaldoxime (IAOx)-related auxin side pathway; not main route
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Not recommended as a primary target for engineering bulk auxin biosynthesis because the review concludes CYP79B2 is not a major contributor to the main auxin pathway and may have species-limited relevance.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Can produce IAOx but is not a major contributor to overall IAA biosynthesis in Arabidopsis
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Double-mutant genetic analysis eliminating IAOx production with only subtle growth defects
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Alternative side-route precursor, not main contributor
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Appears limited to a small number of plant species and unlikely to be a universal auxin biosynthesis gene.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Perturbation may affect specialized metabolism beyond auxin
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CYP79B2 converts Trp to IAOx. 2. Despite eliminating IAOx with CYP79B3 double mutation, plants show only subtle growth defects. 3. Therefore it is not considered a major contributor to the main IAA pathway.

Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: Indole-3-acetaldoxime (IAOx)-related auxin side pathway; not main route
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Not recommended as a main engineering lever for auxin production because evidence indicates only a minor role in IAA biosynthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Supports IAOx formation but is not a major determinant of overall IAA biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Alternative side-route precursor, not main contributor
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Species-limited distribution argues against a universal role in auxin biosynthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CYP79B3 works with CYP79B2 in Trp-to-IAOx conversion. 2. Loss of both removes IAOx but causes only subtle growth defects. 3. Thus it is not a main auxin biosynthetic enzyme.

Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: IAN-to-IAA conversion route; not main auxin pathway
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Not a preferred engineering target for increasing IAA because nitrilase overexpression does not trigger auxin overproduction and the family is thought to function mainly in glucosinolate/camalexin/cyanide metabolism.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Potentially produces IAA from IAN, but evidence suggests nitrilases are not major players in auxin biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant screen for IAN insensitivity; overexpression lacking auxin overproduction phenotype
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Alternative side-route to target product, not main contributor
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Measurement of endogenous IAN vs IAA levels mentioned; no accession reported
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Arabidopsis contains four nitrilase genes; review emphasizes stronger roles in glucosinolate metabolism, camalexin homeostasis, and cyanide detoxification than in auxin biosynthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Engineering may perturb defense and detoxification metabolism
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Nitrilases can convert IAN to IAA. 2. However, overexpression does not cause auxin overproduction. 3. The family is therefore not considered a major contributor to auxin biosynthesis.

Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: IAAld-to-IAA oxidation route; not main auxin pathway
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Not recommended as a primary engineering target for auxin increase because aldehyde oxidase activity is dispensable for major auxin-related development in the cited genetics.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Potentially forms IAA from IAAld, but aldehyde oxidases are probably not major contributors to auxin biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Genetic analysis of aba3 mutants lacking molybdenum cofactor and aldehyde oxidase activities
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Alternative side-route to target product, not main contributor
Pathway_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: ABA3 (molybdenum cofactor sulfurase, required for activity)
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: IAAld accumulation analysis mentioned; no accession reported
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: aba3 mutants lack aldehyde oxidase activity but do not show obvious auxin-related defects or IAAld accumulation.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Manipulation may affect other molybdenum-cofactor-dependent processes
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Aldehyde oxidases can oxidize IAAld to IAA. 2. However, mutants lacking this activity do not show obvious auxin defects. 3. Thus they are not major auxin biosynthetic enzymes in Arabidopsis.

Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: Proposed IPA decarboxylation route to IAAld; not main auxin pathway in plants
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Not supported as a main engineering target in Arabidopsis because counterparts do not appear to play a key role in auxin biosynthesis or development.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Proposed decarboxylation of indole-3-pyruvate (IPA) or other alpha-keto acids to indole-3-acetaldehyde (IAAld)
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Proposed in bacteria, but Arabidopsis counterparts do not appear to be key contributors to auxin biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Comparative biochemical proposal from bacteria and negative/weak genetic evidence in Arabidopsis
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Alternative side-route precursor, not main contributor
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Same as applied species for counterparts; bacterial enzyme discussed comparatively
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Evidence for IPA decarboxylase-mediated auxin biosynthesis is from bacteria; plant counterparts are not major players according to this review.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Could affect central keto-acid metabolism if manipulated
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. IPA decarboxylase can form IAAld from IPA in bacteria. 2. Arabidopsis counterparts are not supported as key auxin biosynthesis genes. 3. The main plant pathway instead uses YUCs to convert IPA to IAA.
